overall.summary: This chapter's quizzes are generally solid, well-grounded in the text, and appropriately intermediate for chapter 8/33. The main remaining issues are distribution skew toward MCQ, a few low-value FILL/TF items, and several questions that unnecessarily re-explain prior-chapter concepts instead of leveraging build-up more cleanly.

missed_buildup_opportunities.explanation: The TF item re-explains FP16 numerical-range failure in a way that is acceptable locally, but it slightly pads a concept already available from prior vocabulary and earlier chapters. The answer could assume familiarity and focus more on diagnosis than on reintroducing the concept.
The MFU answer re-explains the connection to the iron law's utilization term at a fairly introductory level. Given prior buildup, it could be tighter and more application-oriented.

The mixed-precision question explains selective FP32 storage in a somewhat remedial way for a chapter 8 reader who has already encountered precision and optimizer concepts. It would be stronger if framed around a concrete training failure or trade-off instead of re-explaining the basic reason.

build_up.chapter_level_assessment: The chapter mostly leverages prior vocabulary appropriately and does not feel isolated from earlier material. Difficulty is appropriate for chapter 8/33: mostly intermediate systems reasoning with some quantitative interpretation, though a few questions still read slightly more foundational than ideal.

distribution.type_mix_assessment: over-MCQ and under-ORDER relative to target; SHORT is acceptable, TF/FILL are within plausible range but FILL quality is uneven.

per_question_issues.description: This FILL item is basically direct term recall for 'accelerator bubbles' from an immediately adjacent phrase in the section. It tests naming more than systems reasoning or conceptual inference.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace it with an MCQ or SHORT scenario asking how a team would diagnose and reduce idle intervals caused by data-loading or transfer mismatch.

per_question_issues.description: This is a straightforward recall/classification question about which iron-law term is the main engineering target. It is grounded, but a little too direct for a core framework section.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Turn it into a brief scenario with overlapped transfers and measured utilization, asking which lever remains most actionable and why.

per_question_issues.description: The FILL item asks for 'arithmetic intensity,' which is an important term but here is tested almost as a vocabulary blank rather than as applied roofline reasoning. The surrounding sentence makes the target term too obvious.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Convert it into a SHORT or MCQ asking what a low FLOP/byte ratio implies for optimization strategy on a roofline model.

per_question_issues.description: The TF statement about batch size 33 vs 32 and warp quantization is insightful, but the wording makes the intended answer fairly obvious because it tracks the chapter's explicit example very closely.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Make it scenario-based with two candidate batch sizes and observed utilization, then ask the student to interpret the likely cause.

per_question_issues.description: The MCQ answer explains why MFU is better than raw utilization and rebuts one distractor, but it does not engage much with the strongest plausible confusion: why wall-clock-normalized useful FLOPs matter when hardware can be 'busy' doing overhead work.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Expand the answer slightly to contrast useful model FLOPs with hardware-busy-but-unproductive work like padding, recomputation, or stalled kernels.

per_question_issues.description: The distractor 'Optimizer-bound' is weaker than the others because the section's taxonomy is explicitly D·A·M and does not establish optimizer-bound as a parallel class. This slightly reduces the discrimination of the item.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace it with a more plausible confusion such as 'memory-bound kernel stalls' or 'communication-bound synchronization' depending on what the trace evidence is meant to distinguish.

per_question_issues.description: This TF item is pedagogically correct but too easy: the chapter repeatedly states that optimization is iterative, so the answer is close to a direct restatement of the prose.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace it with a scenario asking what new dominant bottleneck might emerge after a successful optimization and why re-profiling is needed.

per_question_issues.description: This is a correct and useful starter question, but it reduces to recalling the named strategy for 'fits in memory but too slow.' It does not yet force much trade-off reasoning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Frame it as a comparison between data and model parallelism under a concrete memory/throughput scenario, asking why one is preferred.

per_question_issues.description: The TF statement is basically the section's headline fallacy stated almost verbatim, so the answer is obvious from the wording and local context. It does not probe much beyond recognition of the chapter's warning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite as an MCQ or SHORT scenario comparing a 7B and 20B model under fixed data budget, asking what failure mode is most likely and why.

per_question_issues.description: This TF item is true and aligned with the summary, but it is fairly easy to answer from common sense plus recent chapter emphasis. It contributes less than a more integrative final synthesis question would.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace it with an MCQ or SHORT asking how a utilization improvement propagates through runtime, energy, and cost in the iron-law framework.
